sentences.text: Saturday afternoon, and often during the week, we went out for military exercise, shooting, hand grenade throwing.
And it was active officers were training us.
They came always from a local regiment.
And they-- the basic instruction.
We had some sergeant who introduced us to Romanian [INAUDIBLE].
But they were only two of the Jewish which were in this cadet formation.
One was a son of a-- [INAUDIBLE] colonel in the Romanian army.
They were [INAUDIBLE].
But in order to come outside, they were warned.
And I.
We were compulsorily selected, if you could call it so.
You had to go.
At the age of 18, if I remember well, 17, 18, there-- every Romanian youngster had to join the paramilitary training.
We-- we drafted in to the cadet force, served after as training officers for our-- the same year.
So we had to train well.
And in the summer, we had summer camps in [INAUDIBLE].
We were sent in different-- that was the royal groups, they called it.
They sent first military training in [INAUDIBLE].
And afterward, we were attending-- my was statistics and so on, in the villages, and the mountain to go around.
They couldn't.
Not.
My father was, himself, military lines educated.
And he said, be healthy and serve in the army.
Yes.

He was first-- in Charlottenburg, he was taken in the German army, where he made the officer courses and was sent to the front in Bosnia, today is Bosnia, where he won, the First World War, the Iron Cross.
Then he was transferred to our home regiment.
This was a 41 k.u.k.
Frontier Regiment, where he served up to the end of the war, fighting with the Russian, who was-- which invaded Bukowina.
And at the end of the war, he returned to our home town, respectively, to my grandfather, maternal side, being in love with my mother.
I remember, in 1936, Baldur von Schirach, the German youth fuehrer, visited Romania.
And when he returned to-- on his way back to Germany, he passed through the Czernowitz, my hometown.
I was selected to act his guard of honor, to present arms in front of him.
He spoke to everybody in the group, a few words.
And he liked how I presented myself.
He didn't know that I'm Jewish, of course.
Oh, yes.
He was in an SS uniform.
[LAUGHS]
Not.
We had to go, you see?
And we only laughed about it.
The Jew, and he was in the guard of honor for Baldur von Schirach.
My late father was very hot for German culture, having studied there and being educated, from childhood, in German culture.
He just could not believe it.
He was reading German papers.
And-- but it started dawning on him, what is coming.
Some friends, Jewish friends, from Germany, which were born in our parts, came running.
Some had-- were mixed marriages and had to leave their wives and come running back.
And they told me, look what happened as a Jew.
It was hard for him to believe that the German would go to such depths.
In the same time, if I may mention this, 1936, my maternal grandfather died.
As I've told you, he was 48 years councilor for a non-Jewish region.
There was only a handful of Jews there and [NON-ENGLISH] The event, the local Nazi group went with the Romanian flag, with a swastika after the [INAUDIBLE] after this, to give him the honor.
It was their old Singer.
They respected him and told you.
Yes.
Yes.
There was the Iron Guard.
There were, in general, there were four large parties.
There was the National [NON-ENGLISH] Peasant Party.
There was the National Liberal, which were not liberal.
And then was the Social Democrats.
And the Iron Guard, which was starting going up and up.
And the Cuzists, Cuza, there was a party of Cuza, who was also-- The difference between the two parties, in order to understand, the Iron Guard were intellectuals.
And they directed the ordinary people, which the uneducated people.
The Cuza party, which came in 1938 to power, couldn't even find people to be in their councilor or this.
They had no intelligentsia.
They were the mob.
Yes.
You see, you had to be.
My father was interested, my mother.
But we brought and had discussion, had a friend, Dr.
Pistener, who was, many years, a representative of the Social Democrats in parliament.
He died in '38.
And there were always discussion, my uncles.
I had an uncle, who was a leader of-- one of the leader of the Bund.
It was a Jewish socialist grouping.
And he was also the leader, the chairman of the Jewish schulferien.
That was to help poor Jewish children, with holiday in the summer, to give them clothes, to give them a possibility to continue schooling.
To tell the truth, I was so inflamed, by what I heard in school, that I was thinking more on Mussolini's line at the time.
But on the other hand, my father's left direction, so I stood between this.
As a Jew, I had no interest in fascism.
But I would have been converted if I would have been a gentile, you see?
We had, every morning, the lecture by one of the professors, mostly a priest, which, of course, was pro nationalist, chauvinist.
Seven.
Oh, yes.
I was friends with all in the class, you see?
As I told you, I was good in-- as a pupil, particularly in sciences.
And I had colleagues, which had a-- a subscription to my homework.
They copied it.
This helped me afterwards.
I don't know if I commented on the deportation.
The officer, who was in charge, one of the officers in charge of deportation, was a school friend of mine.
I will tell your later about him.
Oh, yes, we did.
You see, we were sitting, the Jews, we were sitting mostly together, you see?
I was the only one-- there were three on a bench.
There were two benches, Juden, benches with Jews.
And I was between a Romanian and a German, a so-called German.
It was not forced.
But they separated them for us.
As I told you, they needed me.
I was like them, sporting, going on marches.
We were together in the cadet force.
So they did not find, in me, something, the typical Jew as they thought it, you see?
I was never excluding me, myself, from going with them out a excursion, for instance, tours, which we went.
I was sent to Godollo, it was, in '35, if I remember well.
There was a jamboree in Godollo, in Hungary.
I went with them, the only Jew.
And to show you, we usually got, on the day of Rosh Hashanah, our books from the school library.
If not, you had to buy.
My non-Jewish colleagues, usually, they were-- put them aside for me, so that I could-- but I had to come to collect them.
But they put aside, for me, the books.
I don't know why this happened, always, in Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.
We knew they were pro Nazi.
In particular, Professor Tsopa and Professor Osietuk, which were in high rank-- officer in the Iron Guard.
But to me, personally, they behaved nicely.
I always wanted to study sciences.
And it was a great disappoint to my Latin teacher.
I was the best in class in Latin.
And he was my form master, Dr.
Carp.
He asked me which department I want, because we had science and classics.
And when I told science, he asked me twice, did you think about?
I said, yes, I want to go a scientific way.
You see, all my uncles and my father, all were science.
So I selected-- went the scientific way.
When were you due to leave your grammar school, in what year?
We had the Abitur, that is matriculation or baccalaureate in Romania called.
They took place in June.
Six of our Jewish friends passed, one failed.
This was a very high ratio of failures.
Generally.
Generally, the gentiles, not Jews.
Because we were from all of the schools.
But ours, we had a greater ratio of passes.
I wanted to study engineering.
But it was difficult for a Jew to enter engineering faculty or medicine-- nearly impossible.
If you had the money, and a lot of money, you could go to France, to England.
And-- but I was not one of the richest one.
So I had to inscribe at the University of Czernowitz, and this scientific faculty.
And there I studied.
I studied-- I read maths, physics.
And did you encounter anti-semitism in your early days at university?
Oh, yes, there we had Juden benches, separate.
We had to sit.
We were not many, three Jews in the whole-- my year, reading maths and physics.
One of our professors, Professor Dr.
Nicolescu, he was, afterwards, president of the Romanian Academy of Sciences.
He protested.
And we had a good seat in the front.
We were not many students.
You see, the gentiles didn't go for mathematics.
So we were three.
And one left during the year to go to Japan.
And we had two left.
Not.
We were secretly in Jewish student societies, Zionist student societies, but officially not permitted.
Yes.
My grandfather was a very hot Zionist and always active, like my uncles, parents.
September.
They wanted me to go to, as I said, go in Palestine.
But-- again, a but-- you needed 40,000 leu, in order to go as a sort of capitalist, which like I couldn't afford.
So the Zionist organization collected some money.
And it was a auction, if you could call it so, to find somebody from the candidates to go.
A young chap, a friend of mine, Yehuda Sherman, Little Sherman, then.
Today, he's Yehuda [NON-ENGLISH], a well-known politician in Israel.
He was minister.
He went.
I stood on.
He will come later in my life, again appear.
And would there not have been a possibility of you going to Palestine, then, on one of the Youth Aliyah schemes, without paying so much?
They were not organized, Youth Aliyah.
You see, they were considered mostly German.
And they had to take from German-occupied territory, Germany, Austria, to take out the Jewish youngsters.
And from our towns, we went to [NON-ENGLISH].
I had a cousin who went.
But they were older.
And then, the idea was of, you finish your studies, and it would be better for you.
You come as a--
We had the order.
Yes, I was that one.
I continued.
I had rank and--
We had it.
You seen, there were not many Jews.
And there were those who had compulsory to do it.
They were-- usually, the Jews were carrying all this material.
Or the heavy things were for Jews to carry.
I was one of the happy one, being-- go on to the group, so I had to go on the side.
Yes.
They were usually Saturday afternoon.
Not so in the scientific faculty.
And I had my lecturer, Professor Dr.
Mayer, was the head of the Nazi party in the Bukowina, Herbert Mayer.
Afterwards, when the putsch against the king-- later, it was in '40-- he had to run.
He disappeared.
But I, personally, did not suffer from him.
You see, I was always-- assure him with my work.
I tried to-- not to make trouble for myself.
I did.
It was risky.
As a Jewish student, you risk always to be beaten up.
And particularly, in the juridical faculty, the low faculty, less in the scientific faculty.
Not.
I saw Jews beaten up.
It was the 1st of December.
It was a-- a danger day.
That was a day when the Transylvania was taken over by Romanians.
The Hungarians always ask it back.
Hitler gives them back.
And then they beat up the Jews.
Because they didn't have Hungarians, so they beat up the Jews and broke Jewish windows and looted.
That the educated intelligentsia.
Oh, well.
We were worried?
In particular, I remember it was a case when a Jewish student, Fallik, was killed, by students, by Romanian students.
And I remember, also, they were arrested, some in my school, when I was in secondary school.
They arrested these communists.
They were not really active communists, but they were arrested.
It was in a higher class, Stein, I remember the name.
And at university, again, all the Jews were classified communists.
Particularly if they did not get with the tie and properly dressed, then they were classified as communists and beaten up, straightaway.
They only, themselves, the Jews had to run themselves.
You see, particularly in the low faculty, there was not a pleasant climate.
We were listening to radio, of course, and worried about, in particular, about the state of the Jews in Germany-- Germany and Austria, because we had family in Austria.
And we had to help them and send what we could.
My uncle, there, older brother of my mother, lived in Linz.
And he went by illegal, with illegal boat, from Bratislava, over the Danube, Black Sea, to Palestine, where he was-- he's a police officer.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
We knew about this and run.
We had friends, from my parents, which came and others which were Romanian citizens or born in Romania, which came in running.
Not.
But where to go?
You had to go-- from Romania, you had to go over German-occupied territory.
In Hungary, Czechoslovakia, there was already the Sudentendeutsche and all the German.
And then you had to pass.
If we wanted to go to France, we had to go all over Yugoslavia, Italy, which was also difficult.
It was Mussolini time.
To France or to England was very difficult.
You were not prevented.
But not-- they did not give you an exit permit.
You could get by paying a lot of money and this.
And Romania is a baksheesh [INAUDIBLE].
So you could always pay.
And the Jews paid.
Corruption.
I was called up, because the Romanian army had peasants, many soldiers, but less officers.
So they took us in order-- we were about 20-odd kilometer from the Polish border.
And we were sent, with a unit of soldiers, on the border, to stop the avalanche, which was coming from Poland.
Because General [?
Erich ?] Smigly, the commandant of the Polish forces, they were caught between the German and Russian.
So they started running to Romania, their whole government, Jews, from Lviv, from another town came.
And we had to bring them in a internment camp.
Most of them soldiers and Jewish, a lot of Jewish.
It was Rosh Hashanah time.
And I remember, particularly, one family which I stopped.
And it was a family, [NON-ENGLISH], I remember.
They were from Lviv, a rich store owner.
And they came, in the-- in their own car.
It was father, mother, a boy who had-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was ick on-- what's, how it's called?
A yellow face all-- Jaundice.
Jaundice, that is.
And a lovely daughter, of course.
And I didn't want to send them internment camp and this.
So I gives them the address of a Jewish family.
They were from-- she was really from Lviv.
And so to go there and to let her in towards them.
And this direction, I give the pass.
It was control checked.
You see, you didn't think about danger.
You had to help.
It was not officer.
I was still cadet.
But we had the intelligence.
Because they were peasants, you see?
We That a company.
We were 36 soldiers.
And we had a section of the border to control, to check.
Yes.
Yes.
But a short time afterwards, we were returned to our studies, more military training.
And we-- that went on, after, till March.
I couldn't tell you exactly the date, when we were sent to officer courses, those who had the right, which had passed the cadet course and this.
We were sent to-- we were sent to Bacau.
There were check-up, medical.
Jews were refused, because, as the Romanian said, something was missing on their weight.
In my documents, I was, in faith, in religion, it was written, Orthodox.
Knowing that, in our school, were no Jews, so automatically, they put Orthodox.
My father was officer in the reserve.
So I was, more or less, kosher.
And I was sent to-- as a mathematician, I was sent to Craiova, to officer school, artillery.
Not.
We were officially continuing our studies.
They took into account that we were there.
We had to pass examination, of course.
I returned twice, for examination, from the unit.
And I came on the 27 of June, in the late afternoon, back, already as sort of officer, a warrant officer, the highest rank which was a special intelligence strip we had, that we were officers.
After three months, we would have been permitted to wear the officer uniform.
They were Russian at the time. 1940, this part was under the Russian, Soviet.
